answer:
Several works in the Falsafa corpus engage explicitly with the question of slavery, treating it as a legal institution (primarily in ancient Indic Smṛti texts), an economic and social system (in 19th-century French political economy), or a moral/political phenomenon to be analyzed, critiqued, or abolished.[^1][^2]

### Ancient Indic Legal Texts (Smṛtis)
These Dharmaśāstra texts define slavery (*dāsa*-tva), its origins, types (e.g., born in the house, purchased, self-sold), caste restrictions (e.g., no Brahmin slavery), manumission conditions, and duties of slaves vs. free workers.[^3]

- **Kātyāyana Smṛti**: Chapter 63 systematically outlines the origins of slavery (voluntary surrender of independence, likened to a wife's status), caste hierarchies (applicable to Kṣatriya, Vaiśya, Śūdra but never Brahmins), prohibitions (e.g., no enslaving Brahmins lest the king's majesty diminish), and rules for manumission (e.g., via oxen for famine-maintained slaves).[^4]
- **Nārada Smṛti**: Chapter 1 details 15-20 types of slaves (e.g., hereditary, for food, self-sold), release mechanisms (e.g., master's grace, saving master's life for a son's share), and distinctions from free workers.[^5]
- **Bṛhaspati Smṛti**: Chapter 1 covers slave origins, work classifications (impure slave work vs. pure worker tasks), and inheritance rules involving slaves.[^6]
- **Manusmṛti**: Scattered across Chapters 8-10: origins of slaves (7 types), property rules (slaves have no ownership), punishments, and offspring of slaves.[^7]

### 19th-Century French Political Economy
Charles Dunoyer and Charles Comte analyze slavery's compatibility with liberty, its historical/economic effects (e.g., on population, industry, morals), and transitions to serfdom/wage labor.

- **Charles Dunoyer, *Nouveau traité d'économie: VOL I***: Chapters 7-9 examine slavery as the foundational "economic regime" of sedentary societies (e.g., Hebrews, Greeks, Romans, Gauls), its brutalizing effects on masters/slaves, population decline, and evolution to serfdom in the Middle Ages.[^8]
- **Charles Comte, *Traité de Législation* (multi-volume)**: VOL I critiques slavery's moral/economic impacts (e.g., climate justifications).[^9] VOL IV dedicates ~20 chapters to slavery (*esclavage domestique*): its nature/varieties (Ch. 2), physical/intellectual effects on masters/slaves (Chs. 3-4), moral corruption (Chs. 6-11), governmental influences (Chs. 15-17), abolition arguments (Ch. 21), and inequality legacies (Chs. 22-23).[^10]

Poetic works like Allama Iqbal's *Bang-e-Dara* reference slavery metaphorically or historically (e.g., Islamic liberation from "chains"), but do not systematically engage its philosophical/legal questions.[^11]

[^1]: Search results across "slavery," "slave," etc., yield ~200 hits concentrated in these works; corpus has no comprehensive modern abolitionist treatises beyond Comte/Dunoyer.

[^3]: *Nārada Smṛti*, [Ch. 1](/works/unknown-naradasmrti-7650d2/01/translation/).
[^4]: *Kātyāyana Smṛti*, [Ch. 63/translation/#p-fbeb35](https://falsafa.thothica.com/works/unknown-katyayana-smrti-1e06d2/63/translation/#p-fbeb35).
[^5]: *Nārada Smṛti*, [Ch. 1/translation/#p-78d1bb](https://falsafa.thothica.com/works/unknown-naradasmrti-7650d2/01/translation/#p-78d1bb).
[^6]: *Bṛhaspati Smṛti*, [Ch. 1/translation/#p-b30726](https://falsafa.thothica.com/works/unknown-brhaspati-smrti-0fd070/01/translation/#p-b30726).
[^7]: *Manusmṛti*, [Ch. 8/translation/#p-5be08b](https://falsafa.thothica.com/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/08/translation/#p-5be08b).
[^8]: Charles Dunoyer, *Nouveau traité d'économie: VOL I*, [Ch. 7/translation/#p-6c6409](https://falsafa.thothica.com/works/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-i-6da8ce/07/translation/#p-6c6409).
[^9]: Charles Comte, *Traité de Législation: VOL I*, [Ch. 2/translation/#p-56a86f](https://falsafa.thothica.com/works/charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-i-7e62a2/02/translation/#p-56a86f).
[^10]: Charles Comte, *Traité de Législation: VOL IV*, [Ch. 2/translation/](https://falsafa.thothica.com/works/charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-iv-bca040/02/translation/).
[^11]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 3*, [Ch. 4/translation/#p-6bee14](https://falsafa.thothica.com/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/04/translation/#p-6bee14).
